message.content:
PLAYER: "Scrodinger" (multiple accounts).
RATINGS: chess.com rapid 2010 (main: scrodingxxf) & 2000 (alt); lichess blitz 1707, rapid 1754, classical 1771; chess.com blitz best 1612, currently lower; bullet ~1150.
TACTICS/PUZZLES: chess.com tactics 2073-2212 (a genuine strength), lichess puzzles 2073, Puzzle Rush 48/51.
ESTIMATED FIDE OTB: ~1600-1700. Has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet.
REPERTOIRE — White: 1.e4 player. vs 1...e5 Italian Game (wants to add Ruy Lopez); vs Sicilian plays Open Sicilian (inconsistent); vs French Tarrasch 3.Nd2; vs Caro-Kann Advance 3.e5.
REPERTOIRE — Black: vs 1.e4 Sicilian (various lines, primary weapon but inconsistent — wants to settle on Najdorf); vs 1.d4 Nimzo-Indian (E43); occasional Scandinavian/Caro (secondary, should drop).
STRENGTHS: strong tactical eye; good rapid calculation; very fast improver (+400 in 18 months); aggressive, plays for the win.
WEAKNESSES (priority order): (1) Black win rate 32% vs White 48% — Black repertoire inconsistent; (2) king safety — loses to coordinated middlegame attacks; (3) blitz 500+ pts below rapid — calculation not yet automatic under time pressure; (4) middlegame transition after the opening — loses the thread converting to a positional game; (5) endgame technique likely undertrained; (6) no stable OTB rating.
GOAL: become a very strong player on the path toward titles (CM -> FM -> IM -> GM over years). Wants a personalized course/resource curriculum.


YOU ARE AN ADVERSARIAL FACT-CHECKER for chess resources. Category: "Black vs 1.d4 / 1.c4 / 1.Nf3".

Here are researched resources as JSON:
[
 {
  "name": "Lifetime Repertoires: Nimzo-Ragozin",
  "rtype": "Online Course",
  "provider": "Chessable (IM Christof Sielecki & FM Daniel Barrish)",
  "url": "https://www.chessable.com/lifetime-repertoires-nimzo-ragozin/course/17354/",
  "cost": "USD 99.99 (video + book); ~USD 49.99 book only (frequent sales)",
  "level": "1800-2300",
  "why": "This is the single most coherent fix for Scrodinger's weakest area (Black win rate 32% vs 48% White, weakness #1). It is one connected system: the Nimzo (E43, his current main line) vs 3.Nc3, and the Ragozin (3...d5 4.Nc3 Bb4) as the explicit answer to the 3.Nf3 move order he needs a backup for — plus Catalan, London, Trompowsky, Torre. Sielecki's spaced-repetition trainer attacks his other gap, the inconsistent/unsettled Black repertoire, by drilling one tree to automaticity.",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "2-4 months to learn core; ongoing SRS review"
 },
 {
  "name": "A Complete Opening Repertoire for Black after 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6! Volume 1: Nimzo-Indian",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Thinkers Publishing (GM Dariusz Swiercz)",
  "url": "https://thinkerspublishing.com/product/a-complete-opening-repertoire-for-black-after-1-d4-nf6-2-c4-e6-volume-1-nimzo-indian/",
  "cost": "~USD 34.95 print; ~USD 19.99 Forward Chess ebook",
  "level": "2000-2400",
  "why": "Built on the same 1...Nf6 2...e6 move order Scrodinger already uses with his Nimzo (E43), Vol 1 gives a deep, aggressive Nimzo that suits his 'play for the win' style and strong tactical eye. The 352-page depth supports his GM-track goal where memorizing lines is not enough — Swiercz explains the resulting middlegame plans, directly addressing his weakness #4 (losing the thread after the opening).",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "~3 months for Vol 1"
 },
 {
  "name": "A Complete Opening Repertoire for Black after 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6! Volume 2: Ragozin & Anti-Catalan",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Thinkers Publishing (GM Dariusz Swiercz)",
  "url": "https://thinkerspublishing.com/product/a-complete-opening-repertoire-for-black-after-1-d4-nf6-2-c4-e6-volume-2/",
  "cost": "~USD 39.95 print; ~USD 19.99 Forward Chess ebook",
  "level": "2000-2400",
  "why": "This is the exact companion that closes the hole in his repertoire: when White avoids the Nimzo with 3.Nf3 (or 3.g3 Catalan), Vol 2 supplies the Ragozin (3...d5 4.Nc3 Bb4) and Anti-Catalan. Together with Vol 1 it forms the same Nimzo+Ragozin pairing the orchestrator asked for, in book form for the player who wants GM-level rigor toward CM/FM and beyond.",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "~2-3 months for Vol 2"
 },
 {
  "name": "Lifetime Repertoires: Nimzo-Queen's Indian (Part 1 & 2)",
  "rtype": "Online Course",
  "provider": "Chessable (GM Benjamin Bok)",
  "url": "https://www.chessable.com/lifetime-repertoires-nimzo-queens-indian-part-1/course/154635/",
  "cost": "~USD 99.99 each part (book+video); book-only cheaper on sale",
  "level": "1800-2400",
  "why": "An alternative one-stop system if Scrodinger prefers the Queen's Indian (...b6) over the Ragozin as his anti-3.Nf3 backup. It keeps his Nimzo vs 3.Nc3 and adds the Queen's Indian vs 3.Nf3 from the same 1...Nf6 2...e6 move order, and explicitly covers Trompowsky, Jobava London, BDG and 2.Nc3 sidelines — closing the move-order gaps that currently make his Black results inconsistent (weakness #1).",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "3-5 months across both parts"
 },
 {
  "name": "Playing the Nimzo-Indian: A Dynamic Repertoire",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Quality Chess (GM Renier Castellanos)",
  "url": "https://www.newinchess.com/playing-the-nimzo-indian",
  "cost": "~USD 32.99 print; USD 19.99 ebook",
  "level": "1900-2400",
  "why": "A 440-page active, counterattacking Nimzo repertoire after 3.Nc3 Bb4 that matches his aggressive, win-seeking style and rewards his tactical strength. Its companion 'Beating the Queen's Gambit – Indian Style!' covers the 3.Nf3 d5 problem, so the pair gives a complete Quality Chess solution if he prefers prose-heavy explanation over Chessable drilling.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "~3 months"
 },
 {
  "name": "Keep It Simple for Black",
  "rtype": "Online Course",
  "provider": "Chessable / New In Chess (IM Christof Sielecki)",
  "url": "https://www.chessable.com/keep-it-simple-for-black/course/92793/",
  "cost": "~USD 99.99 (book+video); book-only ~USD 49.99",
  "level": "1400-2100",
  "why": "A pragmatic fallback if the Nimzo+Ragozin learning load proves too heavy alongside his other priorities. One streamlined QGD-based system answers 1.d4, 1.c4 AND 1.Nf3 in a single tree, which would immediately stop the move-order losses behind his 32% Black score. Lower theory burden also helps his weakness #3 (calculation not yet automatic under time pressure) by reducing how much he must recall in blitz.",
  "priority": "Optional",
  "time": "1-2 months (lighter than the Nimzo sets)"
 },
 {
  "name": "The Nimzo-Indian Defence (Grandmaster Repertoire)",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Quality Chess (GM Michael Roiz)",
  "url": "https://qualitychess.co.uk/products/1/283/the_nimzo-indian_defence_by_michael_roiz/",
  "cost": "~USD 29.95 print; ~USD 19.99 ebook",
  "level": "2100-2500",
  "why": "A reference-grade, positionally principled Nimzo built around 3...Bb4 for when Scrodinger pushes past 2100 toward FM/IM and needs elite-level mainline coverage. Its emphasis on fighting for the initiative and active piece play complements his attacking instincts while deepening the positional understanding he loses after the opening (weakness #4).",
  "priority": "Optional",
  "time": "ongoing reference, ~4 months to absorb"
 },
 {
  "name": "Nimzo-Indian Defense lecture series (Chess Openings Explained / Grandmaster's Choice)",
  "rtype": "YouTube Channel",
  "provider": "Saint Louis Chess Club (NM Caleb Denby, IM Dorsa Derakhshani, GM Robert Hungaski)",
  "url": "https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCM-ONC2bCHytG2mYtKDmIeA",
  "cost": "Free",
  "level": "1600-2200",
  "why": "Free, idea-first lectures (the f3 Nimzo, doubled-pawn structures, the Qc2 main line) that teach the typical Nimzo middlegame plans rather than raw moves. This directly targets his weakness #4 (transitioning from opening to a positional middlegame) and his king-safety weakness #2, since Nimzo structures hinge on understanding when to allow doubled c-pawns and where the king belongs.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "ongoing, ~30-60 min per video"
 },
 {
  "name": "Nimzo-Indian Defense theory playlist",
  "rtype": "YouTube Channel",
  "provider": "Hanging Pawns",
  "url": "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xX_bnk7nKNg",
  "cost": "Free",
  "level": "1600-2200",
  "why": "A free, structured walkthrough (Introduction, Qc2/Classical, Main Line) that is the ideal low-cost first pass before committing to a paid Nimzo course. It lets Scrodinger confirm the Nimzo (E43, already his line) is the system he wants to settle on, addressing the 'inconsistent Black repertoire' root cause of weakness #1 before he spends on Chessable.",
  "priority": "Optional",
  "time": "~5-8 hours total"
 }
]

For EACH resource: use WebSearch/WebFetch to confirm it genuinely exists, the author/provider is correct, it is currently available, and the price is roughly right. Correct any wrong URL/price/author/level. Set verified=true ONLY if you confirmed it real and accurately described; otherwise verified=false with a note. Default to skepticism: if you cannot confirm a specific course/book really exists, mark verified=false and confidence=low. Drop obvious hallucinations entirely. Also re-check that each "why" is genuinely personalized to Scrodinger and tighten it if weak. Reputable, well-known resources (Silman, Dvoretsky, Vukovic, Naroditsky, Saint Louis CC, established Chessable bestsellers) can be high-confidence. Return the cleaned list.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Lifetime Repertoires: Nimzo & Bogo-Indian Defense [PGN] - Sciarium","url":"https://sciarium.com/file/672883/"},{"title":"Lifetime Repertoires - Chess Courses","url":"https://www.chessable.com/courses/s/lifetime/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Chessable Lifetime Repertoires: Nimzo-Ragozin course:

The course is authored by IM Christof Sielecki (aka ChessExplained) and FM Daniel Barrish. At its heart is an up-to-the-minute examination of the Nimzo-Indian, considered the most challenging mainline response to 1.d4 for elite players. The Ragozin is presented alongside it to deal with 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6 3.Nf3 d5 4.Nc3 Bb4.

The course also tackles the increasingly-popular Catalan along with the London, Veresov, Trompowsky, Torre, Colle and all the deviations you need to be fully covered. As an added bonus, the authors tackle 1.Nf3 by forcing White into your familiar realm.

IM Sielecki and FM Barrish are two of Chessable's most experienced authors who have amassed thousands of students between them. IM Sielecki is better known as the YouTube streamer ChessExplained and created the award-winning Keep It Simple series, while FM Barrish is one of Chessable's most trusted authors who kicked off the Short & Sweet series and collaborated with IM John Bartholomew on Essential Theory: The London System.

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about Dariusz Swiercz's book:

## A Complete Opening Repertoire for Black after 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6 – Volume 1: Nimzo-Indian

This is a book by Dariusz Swiercz published by Thinkers Publishing, with ISBN 9789464201215 and 352 pages.

### About the Book

The book helps readers understand the instructive patterns of the Nimzo-Indian Defense, packed with new ideas and methods that consider the different ways White has at his disposal. Swiercz wrote this book as part of a three-volume series covering the whole repertoire for Black after 1.d4, following his previous work on the Ruy Lopez.

### Author Background

Dariusz Swiercz was born in 1994 in Tarnowskie Gory, Poland, and became one of the youngest to receive the Grandmaster title at the age of 14 years and 7 months. This is his third book for Thinkers Publishing, after 'The Modernized Ruy Lopez' (a two-volume edition published in 2021).

### Reception

Swiercz strikes a good balance between presenting the theoretical state of the lines with numerous novelties and explaining the ideas behind them, creating a repertoire intended to stay relevant for many years.

content.text: Both confirmed. The first is by IM Christof Sielecki and FM Daniel Barrish — but note the search hints it covers "Nimzo & Bogo-Indian" in one listing; the actual Nimzo-Ragozin course (17354) is confirmed real. Let me verify the rest.

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about this book:

## Swiercz Complete Opening Repertoire for Black after 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6 Volume 2

This is "A Complete Opening Repertoire for Black after 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6! - Volume 2 - Ragozin and Anti-Catalan" by Dariusz Swiercz, published by Thinkers Publishing in 2023.

### Content Overview

GM Dariusz Swiercz explains in a methodological way how to solve opening issues when White proceeds with 3.Nf3 and g3, having picked some aggressive options with the Ragozin and bringing the ever popular Catalan to a stand still.

The author analyzes the Ragozin (1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6 3.Nf3 d5 4.Nc3 Bb4) and the Anti-Catalan (1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6 with 3.Nf3 and 3.g3 lines).

### About the Author

Dariusz Świercz is a Polish-American Grandmaster and experienced repertoire author who was the youngest Polish player to attain the prestigious Grandmaster title at the age of 14 years and 7 months.

### Availability

The book is available from multiple retailers including Thinkers Publishing (the publisher), Forward Chess, Amazon, Chess.co.uk, and other chess book retailers.

Based on the search results, I found information about the Chessable Lifetime Repertoires Nimzo-Queen's Indian by Benjamin Bok:

Benjamin Bok is a leading Dutch grandmaster turned chess streamer whose peak rating of 2645 FIDE put him at the top 0.01% of chess players. He has an impressive 62% win rate with the Nimzo-Indian.

The course series consists of two parts:

**Part 1:** The course focuses on the classical 1.d4 move order, and after 1.d4, you'll mobilize the kingside right away with 1…Nf6 2.c4 e6 3.Nc3 Bb4, soon followed by …O-O.

**Part 2:** This is available on Chessable as a science-backed chess training app to study openings, tactics, strategy and endgames.

There's also a condensed version: Short & Sweet: Bok's Nimzo-Queen's Indian is a sampler from Lifetime Repertoires: Nimzo-Queen's Indian - Part 1 & Part 2. This version wastes no time in landing the first hit — busting the position open with pawn levers, invading with dynamic pieces, and sacrificing material to back the opponent against the wall.

Playing the Nimzo-Indian: A Dynamic Repertoire [PDF] - Sciarium","url":"https://sciarium.com/file/665241/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about "Playing the Nimzo-Indian" by Renier Castellanos published by Quality Chess:

## Book Overview

"Playing the Nimzo-Indian" is a 440-page book published by Quality Chess on August 21, 2024, by Renier Castellanos.

## Content and Scope

The book offers a complete repertoire for Black after 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6 3.Nc3 Bb4, with an active, counterattacking emphasis on the choice of lines. The book features bulletproof analysis, numerous novelties, and deep explanations of plans and concepts.

## About the Author

Renier Castellanos is a newly crowned grandmaster who earned his final GM norm shortly after completing work on this book. He is a lifelong Nimzo-Indian aficionado and an experienced chess trainer from Spain.

## Companion Volume

The forthcoming companion volume, "Beating the Queen's Gambit – Indian Style!", will complete the coverage of 1.d4 Nf6 from Black's perspective by covering White's alternatives on moves 2 and 3, with 2.c4 e6 3.Nf3 d5 the biggest topic.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about "Keep It Simple for Black Sielecki Chessable QGD repertoire 1.d4":

## Keep It Simple for Black - QGD Repertoire

IM Christof Sielecki, aka 'Chessexplained', presents a complete solution to chess players of all levels for their opening choices with the Black pieces in this book.

### QGD as the Main Defense against 1.d4

The recommendation against 1.d4 is the classical reply 1...d5. When you consider playing 1...d5, you need first to decide how to answer White's main option 2.c4, the Queen's Gambit. The suggestion is to play 2...e6, the 'Queen's Gambit Declined' (QGD).

Playing 1...d5, based on the QGD, is considered one of the best replies to 1.d4 and is regularly played by the best players in the world. Black fights for the centre and after 1...d5 2.c4 e6 prepares kingside castling.

### Course Structure

The course contains 6 chapters covering the Caro-Kann, 7 chapters about the QGD and the other 1.d4 lines, and 4 chapters covering other first moves, mostly 1.c4 and 1.Nf3.

You'll learn to fight 1.d4 and 1.c4 or 1.Nf3 with the Queen's Gambit Declined, using the early a6-variation favoured by Magnus Carlsen.

It was voted the best opening course on Chessable by the users of the most popular chess learning platform.

content.text: Important correction: "Playing the Nimzo-Indian" by Castellanos is published by Quality Chess, NOT New In Chess — the URL provided (`newinchess.com/playing-the-nimzo-indian`) is wrong. Correct URL is the Quality Chess one. Also it covers 1...e6 (not specified as a-line). Keep It Simple for Black is confirmed (course 92793). Note KIS for Black uses QGD with early ...a6, and pairs QGD against 1.d4/1.c4/1.Nf3 plus Caro-Kann vs 1.e4 — the "why" framing is fine. Now Roiz and the YouTube channels.
